# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Movies about unusual sexual fetishes

**Score:** 46
**Body:** I'm not talking about Tarantino flicks. It doesn't have to be the main focus of the story. Birthday Girl(with Nicole Kidman) is a good example, altrough bdsm is not a rare fetish, at least not anymore. Ichi is another one I liked

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Crash (1996)
  - Titane (2021) - another body horror that focuses on cars and sexuality
2. Not in a position to judge unusual versus normal, but Secretary (2002) has it's share of kink.  Plus: Maggie Gyllenhaal.
  - Secretary was great!
3. *Hellraiser* technically...
4. Tickled (2016) - It's a documentary.
5. The piano teacher
6. Videodrome (1983) is a horror/sci-fi movie that takes BDSM to the next level. It's directed by David Cronenberg and stars James Woods and Deborah Harry. And yes, there's a lot of gratuitous sex and violence.
7. The Cook, The Thief, His Wife And Her Lover(1989)
8. Secretary (2002) A timid young woman starts working for a demanding lawyer and finds a new way to deal with her self-harming urges.

Blue Velvet (1986) The discovery of a severed human ear found in a field leads a young man on an investigation related to a beautiful, mysterious nightclub singer and a group of psychopathic criminals who have kidnapped her child.

Fetish (2022) Young Anabel falls for wealthy Oskar, unaware of his dark sexual desires. Their relationship spirals as Oskar's fetishes intensify, culminating in tragedy despite Anabel's initial willingness to explore.

Lars and the Real Girl (2007) A delusional young man strikes up an unconventional relationship with a doll he finds on the Internet.

## 2. “Descending into Chaos” movies?

**Score:** 25
**Body:** I love movies where the lead character is in a situation that is descending into chaos and spiraling out of control.

Some examples include After Hours, The Taking of Pelham 123, Fargo, Brazil, and Falling Down.

  
Recommendations?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Mother !!!!!!! This should be top of the list
2. Trainspotting (1996)

Requiem for a Dream (2000)

Uncut Gems (2019)
3. Climax. 2018, Directed by Gaspar Noé. 

After spiking the punch bowl, everyone slowly descends!
4. Descent - watch original UK ending. A movie about descending into the earth and madness.
5. Project X
6. The Machinist


Taxi Driver


The Deer Hunter




Fight Club
7. Apocalypse Now
8. Layer Cake (2004)
9. Boogie Nights
10. Any Alien movie

## 3. What is your Chicken Soup Movie(s)?

**Score:** 22
**Body:** I’m looking for movie recommendations that are your chicken soup for your soul watches. Something to watch if you need a pick me up when you’re feeling a little bluesy. 

My recommendation would be Stranger Than Fiction (2006). If you haven’t seen it, don’t rule it out just because of Will Ferrell. He shows some real range and warmth as the main character. Shockingly good cast too. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Kiki's Delivery Service
2. Forgetting Sarah Marshall - it's funny, and the damaged man finds himself.
3. Ratatouille.
4. The Martian. Because as stressful as my life might be, at least I’m not stuck on Mars
5. Paddington
6. Shrek
7. Truman show, Practical magic, 10 things I hate about you, Kiki's delivery service,  spirited away, ponyo, howls moving castle
8. Raising Arizona.
9. That Thing You Do
10. Under the Tuscan Sun

## 4. Great film directed by a woman

**Score:** 22
**Body:** I was looking for a film to watch tonight and this seem like a fun way to help me decide wich one. Did you know any great film directed by a woman? Let me know. I don't have a real genre preference but maybe horror and thriller wil be more of my taste. But really suggest what you want. I hope you guys can help me

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Kathryn Bigelow has done some good thrillers. Blue Steel might be what you’re looking for. American Psycho was directed by a woman. If I Had Legs I’d Kick You has a female director (Mary Bronstein) and is very tense. I also love Mississippi Masala (directed by Mira Nair) and more recently, Sorry, Baby by Eva Victor.
  - I would like to add Point Break to this list
  - She's got horror covered too with Near Dark.
2. Winter's Bone, directed by Debra Granik. First starring role, I believe, for Jennifer Lawrence. Nominated for several Academy Awards, including best picture.
3. The Piano
4. Near Dark by Kathryn Bigelow
5. A League of Their Own, directed by Penny Marshall.
6. Lisa Frankenstein. Directed by Zelda Williams.
7. Little Women by Greta Gerwig

The Namesake by Mira Nair
8. [Ravenous](https://letterboxd.com/film/ravenous/) by Antonia Bird. A Western/Horror starring Guy Pearce and Robert Carlyle. I've always enjoyed this movie, it really stands out among most modern horrors and westerns.

## 5. Older slap stick movie reccs for 11yo boy?

**Score:** 16
**Body:** I am trying to compile a list of movies that will hold his interest, any era, but I have been trying 80's of late. He loves Home Alone and we just watched Christmas Vacation and he is quoting it already. He sort of liked Vacation (I fast forwarded through the adult scenes), and The Great Outdoors. Any suggestions are welcome. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Three Amigos
2. He's about the age that my son fell in love with Peter Sellers , specifically the Pink Panther movies.
3. I would recommend The Airplane movies,the Naked Gun franchise and both Hot Shots films.
  - Don't forget Men in Tights! Carey Elwes' best role!
4. Spaceballs
5. Monty Python and the Holy Grail
6. The Three Ninjas

Big Trouble in Little China 

The Last Dragon

Kung Pow 

Kung Fu Hustle 

Shaolin Soccer
7. Dumb and Dumber
8. Mel Brooks’ Silent Movie
9. It's a Mad, Mad, Mad, Mad World

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Such a weird franchise.

**Score:** 3731

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Why do so many people talk about how no one talks about this movie.
  - Because it's the most successful film franchise in the world?
2. Millennials when they can’t make a reaction gif from a movie
  - Jokes on you. I dress and act like Spider every day of my life
    - Spider from goodfellas?
3. idk how this franchises existence makes people so mad
  - I think it first stemed from a certain sect of online nerd fandom that is very pissed Cameron won't just go back to making films like Terminator, Aliens, and True Lies. 

The hate really started with Titanic.

Then a lot of them got excited cause of rumors about Battle Angel Alita, than when Avatar came out and didnt deliver a T2/Aliens like fix, and they hated it cause it was more Ferngully than T2, then it had the audacity to be the most successful film ever, people had to rationalize it somehow. 

And lack of cultural impact has been that rationalization ever since. 

Including leading up to the second film's release when haters assured everyone that it was a flash in the pan and would not sniff 1 billion, let alone 2.
  - It's baffling. Like they're technical showcases, fun and light adventure stories without much deep meaning. Why does it have to be anymore than that? Not to mention it could be the kind of event that keeps people going to theaters, which helps everyone.
4. People are framing this as if it is skibidi brainrot with a Hollywood budget
5. This is posted almost daily and I’m just kind of tired of it. Enjoy your karma.

## 2. Which celebrity death hit you personally.

**Score:** 2165

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Alan Rickman is our fav in Harry Potter
2. Phil Hartman
3. Steve Irwin
4. Robin Williams really hit me. Happiest guy on screen, grew up watching him in everything since I was a 90s kid. Jumanji was awesome... His dramatic acting was stellar too. 

Then he died, and the way he died blew me away, it was terrible.
  - His form of dementia is so hard.
5. Bill Paxton. If he was in it, I would watch it.  Legend.
6. Philip Seymour Hoffman 💔
7. Heath Ledger.
8. David Bowie
9. John Ritter. He looked like my dad, and my dad was a big fan of Three's Company growing up.

## 3. Christopher Nolan doesn't understand why The Dark Knight Rises has been seen as a right-wing film

**Score:** 841

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. * Villain takes over a city.
* Uses the populace's anger against billionaires for personal enrichment.
* Discards billionaire allies once ascends to power.
* Makes speeches about how he's "liberating" the citizens from government oppression.
* First official act: release hundreds of criminals from jail.
* Take over the judicial system and puts all his enemies on trial.
* Puts own militia in charge of policing.
* Always keep saying is the common man's savior while in truth is destroying the city.

Jeez, it sounds familiar but I don't recall if it's with the right wing or the left wing parties.
  - Legitimately can't tell if you are describing something modern or the French Revolution.
2. I enjoyed [Eileen Jones’](https://exiledonline.com/the-dark-knight-rises-vs-the-99/) review of the movie back when it came out. 

> Because sure enough, the dreadful working class hordes dressed in sinister motley casual-wear—hoodies and the sorta thing—are manipulated by Bane to take back their city. So the first thing they do is buckle down to releasing all the violent psychopathological criminals in the prison—that’s the first thing protesters always do, it’s Step One in the Social Justice Playbook. Then they go around looting violently and attacking women in fur coats.
  - I never thought of the movie from that perspective, but it’s kind of hilarious to think about. 

This was right in the middle of the Occupy Wall Street protests, and Bane’s group literally occupies and shuts down the Gotham stock market lmao

I don’t think that’s what Nolan was going for, but it is funny to think about
  - Nolans batman films are so contemporary people seem to have collective amnesia and forget that it's supposed to be fucking gotham lmao. Half the city is locked up in prison and the other half are their friends.
    - Someone on here made a list recently of everything wrong with Gotham and it made me realize if the entire city was cast into hell, it wouldn’t even be in its top 10 problems.
3. Bane and his supporters are often seen as left wing groups when they take over Gotham and impose anarchy, with the poor looting the rich homes etc..., and then Batman rallying with the cops to fight them is seen a pro right.
  - Yeah that’s a stretch. Anybody coming to that conclusion was already of that mindset and trying to conform the movie to it.
  - Of course, despite him being a villain, the entire audience basically cheers Bane when he says to Daggett, “And this gives you…power over me?”
4. Was he inspired by the Occupy movement happening? Probably, yeah. Do I think he was making a statement? No. He's making shit up for a comic book movie.

## 4. Favorite movie about cheating on your spouse disguised as a romcom?

**Score:** 471

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Every Hallmark Christmas movie where she goes back to her childhood home for the holidays and meets the local lumberjack/baker
  - "He doesn't like Christmas so its okay if I cheat on him."
2. Sweet home alabama. The dude in New York is basically perfect; there's no reason to go for the guy making lightning sculptures in Alabama. I guess technically, she was cheating on her husband with her boyfriend in new york. But she cheats on her fiance with her her ex, which is more scummy.
  - I just saw that movie for the first time the other day.  I didn't like it.   I feel like the movie wanted me to be on her side, but she was so un-likable.  Besides the relationship stuff you mentioned, she insulted people and outed someone.  We were supposed to give her a pass because she was drunk.  

The whole scene at the wedding??? She just dumps her fiancee there and he just smiles it off.  Then she punches his mother.
  - And like not only is there nothing wrong with him, she's the one lying about her past, and the fact that she was married the entire time. The bitchy future MIL? She was 100% right to be suspicious. 

Patrick Dempsey's guy dodged the bullet of all bullets on that one. Probably why he was so cool with it.

Reese Witherspoon is 100% the bad guy in that movie.
3. Serendipity
  - John Cusack has made a career of being a charming scumbag.
4. The Wedding Planner
5. No one except the Dad is married in *You've Got Mail*.
6. The Notebook is the worst offender

## 5. SLC Punk! (1998): For those who haven’t seen it, Matthew Lillard is great in this.

**Score:** 185

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. [Matthew is a lovely man](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=D0PnPxyT8Xs) who commits 100%.

Fuck Tarantino. He's a good director but a weirdo scum fuck. I'd take 1 Lillard over 1000 Tarantinos any day.
  - He's a great director, but I've never understood why anyone is interested in his opinions on *anything.* Even movies. He says the dumbest shit.
2. I love you guys, don't get me wrong. But for the first time in my life I'm 18 and I can say ***fuuuuuuuuuuuuuck yoooooooooou.***

Genuinely one of my favorite movies.
  - Such a great scene. Also, I love when Shooter McGavin delivers the line, “I didn’t sell out son…I bought in.” 😂
  - That kid is gonna be a hell of a lawyer some day
3. Jason Segel was great too. Almost unrecognizable on rewatch. 
  - You just blew my mind, I haven't seen this movie since high school. Jason Segel is totally the normcore punk, go figure lol
4. Only posers die.
5. Heroin Bob was my fav character in the movie...I gotta rewatch that soon!

Edit: if you love this movie as much as I do check out the movie "The Anarchist Cookbook" great for a double feature!
6. There may not be a bad performance in this movie. Til Schweiger is hilarious in this

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Favourite Denis Villeneuve film?

**Score:** 45
**Body:** Apart from Dune II, Incendies is the one that stays with me the most. It’s devastating on an emotional level. Every reveal felt like a wound reopening, and Villeneuve’s restraint only makes it hit harder. What impresses me is how tightly the film is constructed, each sequence has a precise function in the narrative architecture, yet still carries its own emotional clarity. The way the film moves between timelines without losing tension is remarkable, and the gradual convergence of the two storylines feels both inevitable and overwhelming. The thing, I feel, is less talked about is how important the first scene is throughout the film, it was a jolt. It’s one of the few films where the craft and the feeling are inseparable.


### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. For me it has to be Prisoners. It has what I think is two of the best performances of any of Denis’ films. 
The tone and atmosphere were perfect for the subject matter. Denis is a master of this so it’s not the most profound thing to say about his work but I thought it was particularly good here.
  - Prisoners is a genuine masterpiece. Fully agree.
2. For me, it comes down to Prisoners and Arrival.

Prisoners, to me, is a near perfect thriller; precise, suffocating, and emotionally disarming. It didn’t just make me feel sad or angry the way other films often do; it made me **furious** in a way I wasn’t prepared for. That anger feels deliberately engineered, almost weaponized. When a film manages to provoke in me the very psychological pressure its antagonist exerts on the characters, that’s a rare kind of effectiveness.

Arrival, on the other hand, succeeds at something far more delicate. It gradually unveils a concept that could easily collapse under its own complexity, yet it never resorts to clumsy exposition. Instead, the film guides you, quietly, patiently, until the revelation lands with startling clarity. I’d read the short story years before seeing it, and the ending still hit with the force of a gut punch. It’s one of those experiences where the emotional impact feels surprising, even if you’ve seen it coming.

Both films left me with that lingering sense of having been subtly undone, just in entirely different, but equally unforgettable, ways.
  - I think the thing with Arrival as well is that it takes the process seriously. It fast-forwards in time and skips over plenty of the language-learning, because of course it does, because it has to. But you have the “kangaroo” story and, unlike pretty much any time you hear that repeated, it’s followed by the character saying that it’s not true and she was just using it as a rhetorical device. There’s the scene of her explaining step-by-step what she’s got to do and how she’s got to do it and why it’s going to take as long as it’s going to take.

Most films would hand-wave all that stuff away. I think even most small indie films about science and science-related subjects tend to hand-wave away a lot of the actual *work*. 

This does the opposite, and it’s very much to its credit. I think that’s part of what grounds it, and part of what allows it to get away with the more fantastical elements.
3. Seeing as no one has said it yet, for me it's Sicario.

I think Denis Villenueve is my favorite director in general, so naturally all of his films are absolute top for me. What was probably my first Denis film has stuck as my answer to favourite film in general when asked on the spot.

I just love how there is 15 minute build up of tension, for 2 minutes of action. It was the first action film I saw where the action felt like it was significant. And some of the overhead panning shots, goddamn. 10/10 film for me.
  - Sicario is just perfect
4. *Blade Runner 2049*. The fact that he made a sequel to one of the greatest sci-fi films ever made, and it was as good, if not better, than the original is insane.

That being said, every one of his films I've seen are incredible and unique, and I can 100% see why someone would call most of them their favourite of his.
5. I'm asking for downvotes but I really wasn't taken with either Prisoners or Incendies. Prisoners in particular simply doesn't have the script to sustain the runtime of the film.

I think Villeneuve's films, for me at least, often struggle to land their emotional punches. His style feels documentarian, observant, and always a little distant. Which I have no issues with, to be clear, I just don't think that a script with heavy emotional beats is the best use of his skills. I wouldn't have Villeneuve remake The Elephant Man, for instance.

I think BR2049 leans into his style with a protagonist and a story that's all about isolation, and a lack of identity. And Arrival is the one that feels like an exception to my aforementioned issue.

Plus, the sci-fi settings also let him play to his strengths in terms of visual design.
  - While I loved both of those, I think your observations are pretty accurate.

I definitely agree about BR2049, which is probably why its my favourite film of his. I think the Sicario script works really well with his style too, being my other favourite.
6. Arrival is as close to a perfect movie I can think of. Not only is the technical execution amazing but the concept of Louise and Ian having such different feelings about the death of their daughter because they have such different perceptions of time and continuity makes it emotionally powerful and thought provoking.

I also love how we're perceiving events through Louise's experience giving us a stronger insight into her perception, even though her daughter hasn't even been born yet having this more omnipresent view seeing her whole life gives it much more meaning and realness making everything much more complicated and heart breaking.

## 2. The Sacrifice by Andrei Tarkovsky. My thoughts after first time watching

**Score:** 15
**Body:** Yesterday I watched The Sacrifice for the first time. It’s my first time watching one of Tarkovsky’s movies, and I was amazed by this unique and existential experience!

It was quite confusing to follow. But not in a way I disliked, rather the opposite. It felt more like a mystery. A mystery within a beautiful painting, because it was so beautifully filmed with visually gorgeous scenes. It was very existential, and it really captures a certain existential dread. It also manages to capture a certain dreaminess throughout the whole movie that feels like a trip almost. This comes from the way the scenes are structured, as well as the way people exaggerate their way of talking about things, putting weird energy and emphasis in an uncanny way. I wonder if Tarkovsky took some substances. If he didn’t, he probably already had some psychoactive natively in his brain LOL.

The movie certainly doesn’t scream out its message, but I suspect the name gives away some. Meaning that it’s about sacrifice, and what it means to actually sacrifice something for real. The movie lays out small clues about this here and there. The protagonist talks about needing to stop talking. To stop being a passive philosopher and instead "to do." To put what he preaches into practice, walking the walk, instead of talking the talk... Like most of us, he has never truly sacrificed anything for real. It’s also interesting later when Otto gives away the map and points out “It’s not much of a gift if you haven't sacrificed anything." Not a major detail, but still something that deals directly with this. In the middle of the movie a war breaks out. It’s an existential threat. Something that threatens to completely destroy them all. In some mystic way, the protagonist learns that he can "save this." I suppose that this is a personal revelation for him, perhaps received in a dream. But either way, whether it’s a dream or an actual mysterious mission he gets in the plot, it changes his entire life. He gets a sort of cold shower and he knows exactly what he must do. He’s lived his whole life wrong. And now, for the first time, he can change it, and "save everyone.".

Throughout the rest of the movie, he does this by sacrificing and putting his thoughts into action. He talked early in the movie about how he despises humanity. That he thinks man has preyed upon nature, done violence to it and replaced the natural and beautiful with humanity’s ugly, destructive and sinful power game. With this in mind, I think that when he burns down the house at the end of the movie, he is truly living out his philosophy for the first time. It is an active protest against materialism. He burns down what ties people to the system. The luxury that everyone in the system, and because of the system, is a slave to. ”It’s not much of a gift if you haven't sacrificed anything.", as Otto said.

Another thing he does, which I find very interesting, is that he is unfaithful to his wife and sleeps with their housekeeper. The fact that he confused and sweaty wakes up in a different bed after this occurs strongly suggests it was all a dream. But it doesn't matter. It has still changed him fundamentally, and you understand this because he immediately decides to burn down the house afterwards. This infidelity is of course a sin. But at the same time, he also said something in the movie about how "The world is built on sin.” That sin paradoxically builds the world. That sin is like a necessary component. So this might be a way to show that when you sacrifice things, when you actually do what you think about and talk about, sin is a price you indefinitely pay. You will put your foot in mud sometimes, but you are at least walking. You are living! Damn, this connects perfectly to what he said earlier about sin being a necessary component of life. Cowardice and passivity are not life, courage and action are life. But action inevitably leads to sin, at least sometimes. They are bad actions done in the name of life. He has also probably fantasized about doing this before, and now (perhaps in a dream, perhaps in reality) he does this for the first time and gets an outlet.

I am most likely missing a lot of things. But these are the thoughts I have after seeing it for the first time. If I read another analysis, I might think something completely different. But a truly interesting movie. Will definitely watch more of his stuff! :)

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Cool write up, it's also interesting that you tackled this as your first Tarkovsky where most people (including myself) watch it last. There is a certain narrative closure to his career that this film encapsulates, very interesting to see the ebb and flow of his filmography. The most interesting part of the film to me is how to color is slowly drained from the film until the climax where it comes back. Obviously the house burning scene is an all-timer as well. I desperately need to rewatch this one as I find it to be one of Tarkovsky's most elusive films for me.
  - The reason I chose this as my first Tarkovsky is due to me being swedish, and having Bergman as one of my favorite directors (some well known actors here from his movies and the tone from the small bits I had seen just felt Bergman-esque). Therefore, I figured this would be the easiest entry for me personally.

Wow you're right, that color change throughout the movie is very intriguing. It's so interesting that the color comes back right after the main character succeeded with his "dire mission". Maybe the color slowly losing it's life suggests time running out and / or death impending? There is a from the middle of the movie constant overhanging threat with the nuclear war, and he has a mission where he can, in his own mind, make it return to like it was before (which it does color-wise).

I think the color dying suggests death, and vice versa the colour coming back suggests life - kinda. And is there anything more life-affirming for a philosopher who is overall very much against humanity, materialism and views the human being tied to the system (which the house stands for, with all its implications) as ugly and destructive, than burning down a whole damn house? He has his whole life just talked, being passive, which can be seen as a form of death on your individuality. Now he's alive, and the movie shows it with the color change!

## 3. Thief (1981) is more thematically charged than Heat (1995), and for me, it's the better movie.

**Score:** 8
**Body:** 


While Heat feels like an evolution of Thief, it is only so in scale and spectacle. While I think Heat is a great film, it doesn't really say anything too interesting, it focuses more on the mythical scale. Thief presents us with a main character who portrays the professional tough guy but in reality is just a man traumatized by institutionalization, which has left him incapable of living in society, either the normal one or the underworld. His panic at being subjugated is presented as bravado but is nothing more than a persona he puts on to try to hide his vulnerability. He is impulsive and tries to chew off way more than he can really handle, many times, he could have done the smart thing and shut up, but his ego and fear got in the way. His misfit behavior becomes obvious at the beginning with how he courts his future wife, who ends up accepting him because of her own share of issues.

At the end, we get a big shootout after he blows up his whole life in order to avoid being controlled and institutionalized once again. This sequence is very easy to misread since he comes out on top, but it doesn't take too much thought to realize that he has nothing left, no present or future and has no one to blame but himself and his lack of social skills, which were stunted by being thrown in a cell for a good chunk of his formative years.

Heat is bigger, more thrilling, and shares some themes to an extent, but I would say that it falls very short in the substance department when compared to Thief.

Ultimately, two great movies, but I think Thief is too underrated when talking about Mann's filmography

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. And the cinematography is really incredible. So many beautiful shots, so many creative compositions and uses of color and lighting. There were times when, just like with Barry Lyndon, I paused the movie just to marvel at the visual art on screen. It’s been awhile since I’ve seen Heat, but I don’t remember its framing, compositions, color, etc. being anything special (not bad, just nothing standout). 
2. I also prefer Thief, though I can see why people might disagree. Thief is on a much smaller scale and is more personal, since it doesn’t divide time between the cops and the robbers. The diner scene in Thief is also better, imho. 

Caan’s Frank is seething with barely suppressed anger. He’s only at peace when he’s in the middle of a job. 

Thief is probably my favorite crime movie.

## 4. What makes a director from this or that country? Let’s take Billy Wilder for example

**Score:** 6
**Body:** Bear with me 

He's Austrian, he was born in Austria in 1906.
He moved to Germany in 1926.
Then he moved to France in 1933, where he made his first film.
In 1934, he finally moved to the United States.
He became a U.S. citizen in 1940.

Almost all of his films (except for one) were made after he became a U.S. citizen.
So, is he considered an American director or an Austrian director?

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. This is awfully reductive. Wilder was part of that generation of European emigres who changed Hollywood. Or perhaps I should say made Hollywood. Take away the films of Michael Curtiz, Fritz Lang, Ernst Lubitsch, Robert Siodmak, etc. you have reduced American cinema.

But while they made American movies in the American studio system, they bought their experiences as artists and as exiles to their work. We’re missing something important if we call them American directors without context.

And what about Miloš Forman? Most of his films were made in the United States, but his Czech movies and his experience as an exile are important to understanding his body of work.

It just seems that more is lost than gained in trying to force these directors into one national box.
2. He made most of his films in America, with mainly US or UK casts and with sensibilities that resonated mainly with the US audiences. He lived and stayed there. He is an Austrian-American person, but he is an American director.
  - Exactly, you can’t talk about American cinema without Billy Wilder, or so many other directors born outside of America. It would be so hard to understand within the context of history if we described every hugely influential American director by what country they were born in. It’s about where they were most influential and where they worked.
3. Most databases you encounter online follow the convention of attributing the director's nationality to the director and production company location to the film. That seems to be a well known convention that is not at all disputed. When the director has more than one nationality, both nationalities are mentioned. 

Billy Wilder was a naturalized American citizen. I believe many directors of his era were. In that case it is literally true that he was American. 

So Riddley Scott is British but Alien (1979) is British/American. It's not that complicated.
  - Ridley Scott has always been considered a British filmmaker. He is a knight of the British Crown. He might own properties and run companies in the US but his career formed in the British isles.
4. Wilder is Galitzianer. This is the part of poland that was taken by the Austro-Hungarian Empire with the partition of poland. Since his birth in 1906, with the collapse of the Hapsburgs in 1918, it has become Polish and parts of it Ukraine.  

Galitzianer Jews in the US were treated as part of an enemy state during world war one even if they had left Austria Hungary due to the oppression of ethnic minorities, novelist Henry Roth as a child in New York for instance.

How should ethnic minorities in countries that no longer exist be treated nationally? It raises the question of why we place such a strong emphasis on people's nationalities. 

Should Tony Curtis be thought of as Hungarian when Hungarian nationalists don't think of ethnic jews as truly Hungarian and when the border of Transylvania has moved so that most of it is now in Romania?

Let's not play the game and just enjoy the art.
5. I’d say it depends on what you’re actually writing about. People have many different facets, and if you try to include all of them on the page, it just becomes confusing. You can look into his ancestry if you want to understand where he started out – the same way people say they’re Irish or German rather than simply American when they’re talking about heritage. But otherwise, it seems fair to call him an American director.
6. In this specific case it’s a nonsensical question. There’s plenty of filmmakers where it’s an interesting question (is Alfonso Cuarón counted for Mexico or America?) but Wilder is part of a wave of Central European Jews who fled when they saw writing on the wall with the rise of the Nazis. Douglas Sirk, Ernst Lubitsch, and plenty of other of the most prominent “American” filmmakers of the 40s and 50s were Jewish immigrants from Nazi occupied Europe. 

The same logic applies to Milos Forman, Roman Polanski, and others who made significant films in the Eastern Bloc in the 60s and then immigrated to America when artistic censorship was cracking down. Asking whether Guillermo Del Toro’s American productions have eclipsed his Mexican productions is fundamentally different than asking that sort of question about someone who was forced to flee their birth country for political reasons.
7. What purpose does this categorization serve? Do we think also of people as being of only a single country? Is an individual from Kurdistan not Kurdish simply because the country has not been accepted as sovereign? What about individuals in exile, is Ulysses not Irish simply because Joyce was exiled at the time? If anything, this only serves to reveal how meaningless national borders are in distinguishing individuals. 

It's a pointless categorization that in today's global world serves an even less meaningful purpose. It is further exacerbated in film where large teams with tremendous budgets produce films at a global scale. Take the current Hamnet for instance: a Chinese-born director living in California making a film about British individuals starring two Irish leads: what country has a claim on such a film? Or something like Triangle of Sadness, backed by a pan-European production: is it Swedish solely because the director is born in Sweden?

## 5. What would you guys consider to be some of the greatest use of editing in film??

**Score:** 6
**Body:** Right now, im doing a college course in film post-production and i would love to gain creative insight by watching some movies that u guys think have an especially stellar use of editing.

Whether it be a movie with insanely complicated splices and added effects  to further awe the viewer or a more nuanced film who's editing compliments the mood of the story, I would love to hear your guys' recommendations!!! 

Im open to any genre too btw

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Hitchcock's Psycho - an absolute masterclass, up to the level of making audiences swear they saw things that were actually never on the screen. It is psychology at the highest level and made film history for good reason. Of course, the shower scene is the pinnacle - but the entire film is just relentless genius of editing and camera work.

The French Connection - Friedkin used editing in ways that broke conventions, the feeling of rhythm, the pressure, the hurry, timelines getting subjectively compressed and dilated, it is again a masterclass in editing that makes the story telling much imitated since then and we take those tricks for granted now.

Raging Bull - well, what can one say. It is as astonishing today as it was then, and considering the way it had to actually be shot and to use different film speeds, too... it's a marvel.

2001: A Space Odyssey - maybe most remembered for editing due to that movie history-making cut from the flying bone to the space ship, but the entire film is full of lessons on using editing for impact and story telling, not to mention how editing is synchronised with pieces of classical music.
2. Raging bull is one of the best edited movies ever. I also thought Oppenheimer had excellent editing. Also check out M by fritz Lang. Your professor would love that you chose that...also it wouldn't be fair to mention editing without something like 8 1/2 or Mirror. 
3. It was a bit much for some people, but I loved what Oliver Stone was doing in the 90s, really pushing editing to the limits with big flourishes and contrasting styles. JFK in particular. He turns a 3.5 hour "talky" courtroom drama into an extremely frenetic and stylised thriller. The jumping back and forth in time, mixing black & white with colour, real archive footage with film. Big influence on how Nolan did Oppenheimer.

The Zapruder scene:

[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2nmGS8rVuIM](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2nmGS8rVuIM)
  - Another one of his I love the crazy editing in is Natural Born Killers.
4. If you haven't seen Dziga Vertov's *Man with a Movie Camera*, now is the time. Revolutionized the art of editing. Here is a link:

[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cGYZ5847FiI](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cGYZ5847FiI)

and wiki for character count:

[https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Man\_with\_a\_Movie\_Camera](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Man_with_a_Movie_Camera)
5. All That Jazz has incredible editing and there's even a storyline around editing and how much it can elevate the final product. I won't spoil what it is but the last cut is so cool.
6. Raging bull has been said he multiple times and for good reason. It is such a beautiful film, and my favorite Scorsese. I actually am not a huge fan of Goodfellas, but that must be included. That first scene and his use of freeze frames throughout is really spectacular. After Hours, too.. really anything Thelma Schoonmaker and Marty did.
7. Whiplash is the first film that comes to mind for me. The editing really gives a sense of the feeling behind a scene, whether it’s anxiety (meeting Fletcher for the first time) or exhilaration (ending sequence) or whatever else. Just think of how much footage they probably shot of alllll those instruments during the jazz performances. The editor had to go through each one, figure out which clip was the best to use for each moment of the orchestration, and how long to stay on it, and make sure it made sense so the story wasn’t lost… just incredible editing.
8. I love Apocalypse Now's montages of superimposed imagery at key moments in the film. They perfectly represent a traumatised, broken mind. The opening sequence is, of course, iconic, but I love the subtlety of the final sequence as Willard leaves the camp. An image of an ancient stone head is superimposed underneath the wide shot of the boat, which slowly fades away and is replaced with Willard's own face, which becomes a mere outline in the darkness that seems to merge in and out of the carved icon. The head is "the jungle", the primordial darkness inside every man that Kurtz succumbed to and that still looms large over Willard.
9. Mad Max: Fury Road is peak action editing. The edit provides both clarity and kineticism - something a lot of action fails at. It helps maintain the plot stakes and emotional core of the film without losing the propulsion or tension. Holding those two goals at the same time is a delicate balancing act. 

It won the Oscars for Editing, Sound Editing, Sound Mixing, and Special Effects. That's a clean sweep for all post-production work.
